title: Haile Selassie and the Modern World
subtitle: The Lion of Judah on the World Stage

sections.title: The Rise of Ras Tafari
sections.body: Born Tafari Makonnen in 1892, the son of Ras Makonnen (the hero of Adwa), Haile Selassie rose through the ranks of Ethiopian nobility with extraordinary political skill. He became regent in 1916 and was crowned Emperor in 1930, taking the name Haile Selassie — 'Power of the Trinity.' His coronation was an international spectacle, attended by representatives from across the world. For many in the African diaspora, it was the first time they saw a black king crowned as an equal among nations.

sections.title: The Italian Invasion and Exile
sections.body: In 1935, Mussolini's Italy invaded Ethiopia using chemical weapons — mustard gas dropped from airplanes on soldiers and civilians alike. The League of Nations imposed weak sanctions and did nothing. Haile Selassie fled to exile in Bath, England. In 1936, he delivered one of the most powerful speeches of the 20th century at the League of Nations in Geneva: 'It is us today. It will be you tomorrow.' The speech was prophetic. World War II began three years later. In 1941, with British support, Haile Selassie returned to Addis Ababa and was restored to his throne.
It is us today. It will be you tomorrow.
sections.attribution: — Haile Selassie I, League of Nations, 1936

sections.title: The Reformer and the Revolutionary
sections.body: After the war, Haile Selassie modernized Ethiopia: he introduced the 1955 Constitution, built universities, and made Ethiopia a founding member of the United Nations and the Organization of African Unity (headquartered in Addis Ababa). But he was also an absolute monarch who resisted democratization. In 1974, a military coup — the Derg — overthrew him. He died under house arrest in 1975. His legacy is profoundly contested: to some, the father of modern Ethiopia; to others, a feudal relic who failed to share power. To the Rastafari movement, he is the returned messiah. He remains the most internationally recognized Ethiopian who ever lived.
